confidence.disclosure: Ahmad Baba was not a great traveller — his life is geographically modest, divided between Timbuktu and 14 years of forced exile in Marrakesh. The route is reconstructed from John Hunwick's translation of his autobiographical notice in Nayl al-Ibtihaj and from al-Sa'di's Tarikh al-Sudan. The intermediate stops on the trans-Saharan route to Marrakesh (Taghaza, Sijilmasa) are not securely attested in his own writings and have been listed only where the standard caravan road makes them effectively certain; precision for these is 'period'.

sources: Ahmad Baba, Nayl al-Ibtihaj bi-Tatriz al-Dibaj (c. 1596-1608)
John O. Hunwick, Timbuktu and the Songhay Empire: Al-Sa'di's Tarikh al-Sudan down to 1613 and Other Contemporary Documents (1999)
John O. Hunwick, Mi'raj al-Su'ud: Ahmad Baba's Replies on Slavery (2000)

journey.event: Born on 26 October 1556 in the Saharan oasis of Araouane to the Sanhaja Berber Aqit family of Maliki jurists.
Moved with his family to Timbuktu as a small child, where he began his studies under his father and the scholar Mohammed Baghayogho.
Witnessed the Moroccan Saadian invasion of the Songhai Empire after the Battle of Tondibi, and joined the scholarly resistance to Pasha Mahmud's occupation.
Arrested with about thirty other prominent Timbuktu scholars after refusing to swear allegiance to the Saadian governors; his library of some 1,600 books was confiscated.
Crossed the great salt-mining centre of Taghaza on the trans-Saharan road during the chained march to Morocco.
Reached the northern terminus of the Saharan caravan route at Sijilmasa-Tafilalt en route to Marrakesh.
Reached Marrakesh on 1 Ramadan 1002 AH (21 May 1594) and was held under the Saadian sultan Ahmad al-Mansur.
Eventually permitted to teach and issue fatwas in Marrakesh, including his treatise Mi'raj al-Su'ud against the enslavement of free-born Muslims.
After Ahmad al-Mansur's death and under his successor Zaydan al-Nasir, was finally given permission to return home.
Reached Timbuktu on 22 April 1608 after fourteen years of exile, and resumed teaching for the rest of his life.
Died in Timbuktu on 22 April 1627, leaving over forty surviving works.
